ECMT674-Termproject-Team18

Jinrui Li

2025-04-30

PART A - SIYU HE

1. Load Data and Initial Visualization

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(forecast)
library(ggfortify)
library(ggplot2)
library(tseries)
library(tsibble)
library(zoo)

data <- read_excel("~/Documents/课程文件/3. 674-Economic Forecasting/Term Project/Texas Tax Revenue.xlsx", sheet = "Sales Tax")
saletax_ts <- ts(data$'Sales Tax', frequency = 12, start = c(2003, 9))

Trend Plot

autoplot(saletax_ts) +
  ggtitle("Texas Sales Tax Revenue (Sep 2003 – Feb 2025)") +
  xlab("Year") +
  ylab("Sales Tax Revenue") +
  theme_minimal()

#Note: The data has an upward trend.

Seasonal Plot

ggseasonplot(saletax_ts, year.labels = FALSE, main = "Seasonal Plot of Sales Tax Revenue",
             col = rainbow(30, s = 0.5, v = 0.8), ylab = "Sales Tax")

#Note: The data has seasonal trends.

2. Log Transformation

log_saletax_ts <- log(saletax_ts)

par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot(saletax_ts, main = "Original Sales Tax Series", ylab = "Sales Tax")
plot(log_saletax_ts, main = "Log-Transformed Sales Tax Series", ylab = "log(Sales Tax)")

### Analysis: The original data exhibits large fluctuations in the later period (high variance), while the log-transformed series shows a more stable pattern (reduced variance).

3. Data Cleaning (Before Modeling)

tax.dat <- read_excel("~/Documents/课程文件/3. 674-Economic Forecasting/Term Project/Texas Tax Revenue.xlsx", sheet = "Sales Tax")
sales <- ts(tax.dat[, "Sales Tax"], frequency = 12, start = c(2003, 9))

xdat <- ts(matrix(0, nrow = 258, ncol = 7), frequency = 12, start = c(2003, 9))
xdat[181,1] <- 1; xdat[192,2] <- 1; xdat[204,3] <- 1; xdat[216,4] <- 1; xdat[226,5] <- 1; xdat[237,6] <- 1; xdat[249,7] <- 1

(tr_fit <- tslm(sales ~ trend + xdat, lambda=0))
## 
## Call:
## tslm(formula = sales ~ trend + xdat, lambda = 0)
## 
## Coefficients:
##  (Intercept)         trend  xdatSeries 1  xdatSeries 2  xdatSeries 3  
##    14.081197      0.004123     -0.882466     -1.682987     -4.639842  
## xdatSeries 4  xdatSeries 5  xdatSeries 6  xdatSeries 7  
##    -0.740528     -1.154617     -1.072156     -1.007503
holiday <- xdat[,1]*tr_fit$coefficients[3] + xdat[,2]*tr_fit$coefficients[4] +
  xdat[,3]*tr_fit$coefficients[5] + xdat[,4]*tr_fit$coefficients[6] +
  xdat[,5]*tr_fit$coefficients[7] + xdat[,6]*tr_fit$coefficients[8] +
  xdat[,7]*tr_fit$coefficients[9]

sales_adj <- exp(log(sales) - holiday)

4. Log Transformation (Before Modeling)

log_sales_adj <- log(sales_adj)
dates <- seq(as.Date("2003-09-01"), by = "month", length.out = length(log_sales_adj))
sales_df <- data.frame(Date = dates, Log_Sales_Tax = as.numeric(log_sales_adj))
write.csv(sales_df, "log_sales_tax.csv", row.names = FALSE)

PART B - JOY SCHUMANN

1. Training and test sets

train <- window(log_sales_adj, end = c(2024, 8))
test <- window(log_sales_adj, start = c(2024, 9))

2. Fit ETS/ARIMA model on training data

ets_fit <- ets(train)
arima_fit <- auto.arima(train, seasonal = TRUE, stepwise = FALSE)

3. Check residuals for ETS/ARIMA

checkresiduals(ets_fit)

## 
##  Ljung-Box test
## 
## data:  Residuals from ETS(M,A,A)
## Q* = 29.707, df = 24, p-value = 0.1946
## 
## Model df: 0.   Total lags used: 24
checkresiduals(arima_fit)

## 
##  Ljung-Box test
## 
## data:  Residuals from ARIMA(0,1,1)(2,0,0)[12]
## Q* = 27.84, df = 21, p-value = 0.1447
## 
## Model df: 3.   Total lags used: 24

4. Forecast 6 months ahead from training models

f_ets <- forecast(ets_fit, h = 6)
f_arima <- forecast(arima_fit, h = 6)
arima_forecast_ts <- ts(f_arima$mean, start = c(2024, 9), frequency = 12)

acc_ets <- accuracy(f_ets, test)
acc_arima <- accuracy(f_arima, test)

print(acc_ets)
##                        ME       RMSE        MAE          MPE      MAPE
## Training set 0.0001428483 0.05753977 0.03652514 5.758389e-05 0.2492362
## Test set     0.0051893710 0.02707150 0.02235645 3.405422e-02 0.1475638
##                   MASE        ACF1 Theil's U
## Training set 0.4719118 -0.05043289        NA
## Test set     0.2888496  0.10603174 0.3003932
print(acc_arima)
##                       ME       RMSE        MAE        MPE      MAPE      MASE
## Training set 0.006394385 0.06420888 0.04343998 0.04276191 0.2971094 0.5612529
## Test set     0.023767672 0.03367141 0.02652593 0.15619060 0.1744539 0.3427201
##                      ACF1 Theil's U
## Training set  0.009148398        NA
## Test set     -0.537733326 0.5102777

5. ts for plotting

time_train <- time(train)
time_holdout <- time(test)
time_forecast <- time(f_ets$mean)

6. df for plotting

df_train <- data.frame(Date = as.yearmon(time_train), Value = as.numeric(train), Type = "Training Data")
df_holdout <- data.frame(Date = as.yearmon(time_holdout), Value = as.numeric(test), Type = "Actual Holdout")
df_ets <- data.frame(Date = as.yearmon(time_forecast), Value = as.numeric(f_ets$mean), Type = "ETS Forecast")
df_arima <- data.frame(Date = as.yearmon(time_forecast), Value = as.numeric(f_arima$mean), Type = "ARIMA Forecast")

7. Combine

full_df <- rbind(df_train, df_holdout, df_ets, df_arima)

8. Plot

ggplot(full_df, aes(x = Date, y = Value, color = Type)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.yearmon("2024-09"), linetype = "dashed", color = "grey") +
  ggtitle("Sales Tax Revenue: Training, Forecasts, and Actual Holdout") +
  xlab("Year") +
  ylab("Log(Sales Tax Revenue)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Training Data" = "black", "Actual Holdout" = "blue",
                                "ETS Forecast" = "red", "ARIMA Forecast" = "green")) +
  theme_minimal()

9. Preferred model

preferred_model <- if (acc_ets["Test set", "RMSE"] < acc_arima["Test set", "RMSE"]) "ETS" else "ARIMA"
print(paste("Preferred model:", preferred_model))
## [1] "Preferred model: ETS"

10. Forecast 30 months ahead

if (preferred_model == "ETS") {
  final_model <- ets(log_sales_adj)
} else {
  final_model <- auto.arima(log_sales_adj, seasonal = TRUE, stepwise = FALSE)
}

forecast_30mo <- forecast(final_model, h = 30)

11. df for long forecast

time_full <- time(log_sales_adj)
time_forecast_30 <- time(forecast_30mo$mean)

df_full <- data.frame(Date = as.yearmon(time_full), Value = as.numeric(log_sales_adj), Type = "Historical")
df_forecast <- data.frame(Date = as.yearmon(time_forecast_30), Value = as.numeric(forecast_30mo$mean), Type = "Forecast")

path_df <- rbind(df_full, df_forecast)

12. Plot

ggplot(path_df, aes(x = Date, y = Value, color = Type)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.yearmon("2025-03"), linetype = "dashed", color = "grey") +
  ggtitle("30-Month Ahead Sales Tax Revenue Forecast (Mar 2025–Aug 2027)") +
  xlab("Year") +
  ylab("Log(Sales Tax Revenue)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Historical" = "black", "Forecast" = "red")) +
  theme_minimal()

13. Back-transform forecast

forecast_30mo_levels <- exp(forecast_30mo$mean)

14.Back-transform upper and lower prediction

forecast_30mo_upper <- exp(forecast_30mo$upper[,2])
forecast_30mo_lower <- exp(forecast_30mo$lower[,2])

PART C - JINRUI LI

1. Select 3 Variables

dataset <- readxl::read_excel("~/Documents/课程文件/3. 674-Economic Forecasting/Term Project/Part C /PartC dataset.xlsx")
vehicletax_ts <- ts(dataset$`Motor Vehicle Sales and Rental Taxes`, frequency = 12, start = c(2003, 9))
retail_ts <- ts(dataset$`Retail Trade and Food Services(RSAFS)`, frequency = 12, start = c(2003, 9))
txur_ts <- ts(dataset$`Unemployment Rate in Texas(TXUR)`, frequency = 12, start = c(2003, 9))

3.b Answer: Variables Chosen

  • Motor Vehicle Sales and Rental Taxes (State level): vehicle-related consumption is a significant part of taxable sales.
  • Retail Sales (National level): reflects consumer spending.
  • Texas Unemployment Rate (TXUR) (State level): macroeconomic indicator for consumer income.

2. Log-Transformation

log_vehicletax_ts <- log(vehicletax_ts)
log_retail_ts <- log(retail_ts)
log_txur_ts <- log(txur_ts)

3. Stationarity Test

ndiffs(log_sales_adj)
## [1] 1
ndiffs(log_vehicletax_ts)
## [1] 1
ndiffs(log_retail_ts)
## [1] 1
ndiffs(log_txur_ts)
## [1] 1

Analysis: All three time series need to be differenced once to achieve stationarity.

3.b Answer: Before modeling, all series were reprocessed for stationarity.

-a) Log transformations were applied to stabilize the variance. -b) The ndiffs() function were used to assess unit roots. -c) Each series was required first-order differencing to achieve stationarity before inclusion in the VAR model.

4. First-Order Differencing

diff_sales_adj <- diff(log_sales_adj)
diff_vehicletax_ts <- diff(log_vehicletax_ts)
diff_retail_ts <- diff(log_retail_ts)
diff_txur_ts <- diff(log_txur_ts)

5. Dividing Training and Test Sets

combined_ts <- cbind(diff_sales_adj, diff_vehicletax_ts, diff_retail_ts, diff_txur_ts)
colnames(combined_ts) <- c("sales", "vehicle", "retail", "TXUR")

training_set <- window(combined_ts, end = c(2024, 8))
test_set <- window(combined_ts, start = c(2024, 9))

Note: As usual, we artificially selected data from September 2003 to August 2024 as the training set, and retained 6 months of data as the test set (August 2024 to February 2025)

6. VAR Modeling

library(vars)
var_selection <- VARselect(training_set, lag.max = 12, type = "const")
var_selection$selection
## AIC(n)  HQ(n)  SC(n) FPE(n) 
##     11     11      2     11
var_model <- VAR(training_set, p = 2, type = "const")
summary(var_model)
## 
## VAR Estimation Results:
## ========================= 
## Endogenous variables: sales, vehicle, retail, TXUR 
## Deterministic variables: const 
## Sample size: 249 
## Log Likelihood: 1266.985 
## Roots of the characteristic polynomial:
## 0.703 0.703 0.5526 0.5526 0.4409 0.4409 0.2963 0.06014
## Call:
## VAR(y = training_set, p = 2, type = "const")
## 
## 
## Estimation results for equation sales: 
## ====================================== 
## sales = sales.l1 + vehicle.l1 + retail.l1 + TXUR.l1 + sales.l2 + vehicle.l2 + retail.l2 + TXUR.l2 + const 
## 
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## sales.l1   -0.561637   0.056216  -9.991  < 2e-16 ***
## vehicle.l1 -0.018715   0.016809  -1.113    0.267    
## retail.l1   0.236240   0.393354   0.601    0.549    
## TXUR.l1    -0.080549   0.118103  -0.682    0.496    
## sales.l2   -0.484895   0.056024  -8.655 7.25e-16 ***
## vehicle.l2 -0.010190   0.016813  -0.606    0.545    
## retail.l2   0.385500   0.324075   1.190    0.235    
## TXUR.l2     0.032205   0.111217   0.290    0.772    
## const       0.006843   0.005169   1.324    0.187    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.0776 on 240 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-Squared: 0.3525,  Adjusted R-squared: 0.3309 
## F-statistic: 16.33 on 8 and 240 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16 
## 
## 
## Estimation results for equation vehicle: 
## ======================================== 
## vehicle = sales.l1 + vehicle.l1 + retail.l1 + TXUR.l1 + sales.l2 + vehicle.l2 + retail.l2 + TXUR.l2 + const 
## 
##            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## sales.l1    0.08624    0.27055   0.319  0.75019    
## vehicle.l1 -0.67276    0.08089  -8.317  6.8e-15 ***
## retail.l1   2.35554    1.89305   1.244  0.21460    
## TXUR.l1     0.07043    0.56838   0.124  0.90149    
## sales.l2    0.19582    0.26962   0.726  0.46837    
## vehicle.l2 -0.31312    0.08091  -3.870  0.00014 ***
## retail.l2   1.70849    1.55963   1.095  0.27442    
## TXUR.l2     0.27335    0.53524   0.511  0.61003    
## const      -0.02190    0.02488  -0.880  0.37958    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.3734 on 240 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-Squared: 0.2289,  Adjusted R-squared: 0.2032 
## F-statistic: 8.908 on 8 and 240 DF,  p-value: 1.115e-10 
## 
## 
## Estimation results for equation retail: 
## ======================================= 
## retail = sales.l1 + vehicle.l1 + retail.l1 + TXUR.l1 + sales.l2 + vehicle.l2 + retail.l2 + TXUR.l2 + const 
## 
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
## sales.l1    0.006105   0.013960   0.437  0.66229   
## vehicle.l1 -0.003416   0.004174  -0.818  0.41402   
## retail.l1   0.126919   0.097683   1.299  0.19509   
## TXUR.l1     0.083337   0.029329   2.841  0.00488 **
## sales.l2    0.012590   0.013913   0.905  0.36643   
## vehicle.l2 -0.003129   0.004175  -0.750  0.45426   
## retail.l2   0.019234   0.080479   0.239  0.81132   
## TXUR.l2     0.060420   0.027619   2.188  0.02966 * 
## const       0.003108   0.001284   2.421  0.01620 * 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.01927 on 240 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-Squared: 0.1394,  Adjusted R-squared: 0.1107 
## F-statistic: 4.858 on 8 and 240 DF,  p-value: 1.446e-05 
## 
## 
## Estimation results for equation TXUR: 
## ===================================== 
## TXUR = sales.l1 + vehicle.l1 + retail.l1 + TXUR.l1 + sales.l2 + vehicle.l2 + retail.l2 + TXUR.l2 + const 
## 
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
## sales.l1   -0.041279   0.046991  -0.878   0.3806   
## vehicle.l1  0.006749   0.014050   0.480   0.6314   
## retail.l1  -0.975853   0.328800  -2.968   0.0033 **
## TXUR.l1     0.118362   0.098721   1.199   0.2317   
## sales.l2   -0.043599   0.046830  -0.931   0.3528   
## vehicle.l2 -0.001173   0.014054  -0.083   0.9335   
## retail.l2   0.097842   0.270890   0.361   0.7183   
## TXUR.l2     0.023624   0.092965   0.254   0.7996   
## const       0.001911   0.004321   0.442   0.6586   
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.06486 on 240 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-Squared: 0.1416,  Adjusted R-squared: 0.113 
## F-statistic:  4.95 on 8 and 240 DF,  p-value: 1.102e-05 
## 
## 
## 
## Covariance matrix of residuals:
##              sales    vehicle     retail       TXUR
## sales    0.0060215  0.0005096  0.0001773 -0.0004667
## vehicle  0.0005096  0.1394641  0.0009071 -0.0033655
## retail   0.0001773  0.0009071  0.0003713 -0.0009611
## TXUR    -0.0004667 -0.0033655 -0.0009611  0.0042073
## 
## Correlation matrix of residuals:
##            sales  vehicle  retail     TXUR
## sales    1.00000  0.01758  0.1186 -0.09273
## vehicle  0.01758  1.00000  0.1260 -0.13894
## retail   0.11857  0.12604  1.0000 -0.76891
## TXUR    -0.09273 -0.13894 -0.7689  1.00000

Note: Based on the VARselect() results, we opt for the lag order suggested by SC(BIC), which recommends 2 lags.

7. Using Test Set to Validate Model

predict_model <- predict(var_model, n.ahead = 6)
predict_sales <- predict_model$fcst$sales[, "fcst"]

library(dplyr)
last_real_log_sales <- last(training_set[, "sales"])
predicted_log_sales6 <- cumsum(predict_sales) + last_real_log_sales
true_log_sales_test <- cumsum(test_set[, "sales"]) + last_real_log_sales

errors <- predicted_log_sales6 - true_log_sales_test
rmse <- sqrt(mean(errors^2))
mae <- mean(abs(errors))

cat("Validation RMSE:", rmse, "\n")
## Validation RMSE: 0.05389834
cat("Validation MAE:", mae, "\n")
## Validation MAE: 0.04821945

Note:

  • The VAR model had a RMSE of 0.0514 and a MAE of 0.0410 on the 6-month test set (September 2024 to February 2025).
  • This shows that the model can predict sales tax reasonably well in the short term, so it is suitable for longer-term forecasting.

8. Using Full Data to Forecast 30 Months

full_training_set <- rbind(training_set, test_set)
full_var_model <- VAR(full_training_set, p = 2, type = "const")

predict_model_30m <- predict(full_var_model, n.ahead = 30)
sales_dlog_fcst_30m <- predict_model_30m$fcst$sales[, "fcst"]

last_real_log_sales_full <- last(full_training_set[, "sales"])
predict_log_sales_30m <- cumsum(sales_dlog_fcst_30m) + last_real_log_sales_full
predict_sales_tax_30m <- exp(predict_log_sales_30m)

dates_30m <- seq(as.Date("2025-03-01"), by = "month", length.out = 30)

predict_df <- data.frame(Date = dates_30m, Predicted_Sales_Tax = predict_sales_tax_30m)
head(predict_df)
##         Date Predicted_Sales_Tax
## 1 2025-03-01           0.9266254
## 2 2025-04-01           0.9647836
## 3 2025-05-01           0.9279105
## 4 2025-06-01           0.9386454
## 5 2025-07-01           0.9595617
## 6 2025-08-01           0.9508439
write.csv(predict_df, file = "multivariate_sales_forecast_30m.csv", row.names = FALSE)

9. Plot 30-Month Forecast

ggplot(predict_df, aes(x = Date, y = Predicted_Sales_Tax)) +
  geom_line(color = "red") +
  labs(title = "30-Month Sales Tax Forecast (Multivariate VAR Model)",
       x = "Date", y = "Sales Tax (Dollars)") +
  theme_minimal()

PART D - KRITI MATHUR

Compare Forecasts

# STEP 1: Read in the forecast files
ets <- read_csv("~/Desktop/univariate_sales_forecast_30m fromJoy.csv")
var <- read_csv("~/Desktop/multivariate_sales_forecast_30m（from Jinrui).csv")

# STEP 2: Convert to date format
ets$Date <- as.Date(ets$Date)
var$Date <- as.Date(var$Date)

# STEP 3: Assign fiscal years 
ets$Fiscal_Year <- ifelse(month(ets$Date) >= 9, year(ets$Date) + 1, year(ets$Date))
var$Fiscal_Year <- ifelse(month(var$Date) >= 9, year(var$Date) + 1, year(var$Date))

# STEP 4: Summarize by fiscal year
ets_fy <- ets %>%
  group_by(Fiscal_Year) %>%
  summarise(ETS_Forecast = sum(Forecast))

var_fy <- var %>%
  group_by(Fiscal_Year) %>%
  summarise(VAR_Forecast = sum(Predicted_Sales_Tax))

# STEP 5: Add Comptroller's forecast
comptroller <- data.frame(
  Fiscal_Year = c(2025, 2026, 2027),
  Official = c(43977083, 46211370, 48026569)  # in thousands
)

# STEP 6: Join and compare
comparison <- ets_fy %>%
  inner_join(var_fy, by = "Fiscal_Year") %>%
  inner_join(comptroller, by = "Fiscal_Year") %>%
  mutate(
    ETS_Error = abs(ETS_Forecast - Official) / Official * 100,
    VAR_Error = abs(VAR_Forecast - Official) / Official * 100,
    Closer_Model = ifelse(ETS_Error < VAR_Error, "ETS", "VAR")
  )

# STEP 7: Show results
print(comparison)
## # A tibble: 3 × 7
##   Fiscal_Year ETS_Forecast VAR_Forecast Official ETS_Error VAR_Error
##         <dbl>        <dbl>        <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1        2025    22717081.    23283217. 43977083    48.3       47.1 
## 2        2026    46575223.    48343808. 46211370     0.787      4.61
## 3        2027    47242742.    50942319. 48026569     1.63       6.07
## # ℹ 1 more variable: Closer_Model <chr>

Comparative Analysis：

  • Based on the forecast comparison with official Texas Comptroller projections, the ETS model was more accurate for FY 2026 and FY 2027, with lower percent errors of 0.79% and 1.63% respectively.
  • In contrast, the VAR model performed slightly better in FY 2025, though both models deviated significantly that year. Overall, the ETS model produced more consistent and reliable forecasts, particularly in the medium term.
